subSlides.text: On December 1, 2001, Argentina's government froze bank deposits. Argentines called it the 'corralito' — the little corral — because it trapped their savings. Over the following weeks, the country went through five presidents in ten days. On December 23rd, Argentina defaulted on ninety-five billion dollars in sovereign debt, the largest sovereign default in history to that point.

The convertibility plan, Argentina's monetary framework since 1991, had pegged the peso one-to-one to the US dollar. For a decade it worked: inflation collapsed from thousands of percent annually to near zero. Foreign investment flowed in. The economy modernized. Convertibility was celebrated as a model for monetary stabilization.

The peg's weakness was its rigidity. When Brazil devalued in 1999, Argentine exports became uncompetitive. When the dollar strengthened against other currencies, so did the peso. Argentina could not adjust its exchange rate to restore competitiveness. The only adjustment mechanism was deflation — driving wages and prices down, which meant unemployment up.

Recession began in 1998. By 2001, unemployment had reached twenty percent. The government, unable to devalue and unable to stimulate, cut spending — deepening the recession and shrinking the tax base. The IMF provided a fifty-seven billion dollar support package in 2001. It was insufficient. When bank depositors began withdrawing dollars, the corralito was the government's last resort.

In January 2002, Argentina abandoned convertibility and devalued the peso. The currency fell seventy percent against the dollar within months. Debts denominated in dollars became unpayable in pesos. Banks — which had lent in dollars and held peso assets — were technically insolvent. The financial system collapsed.

Argentina's crisis illustrated the 'impossible trinity' — the impossibility of simultaneously maintaining a fixed exchange rate, free capital mobility, and independent monetary policy. Convertibility sacrificed monetary independence. When external shocks required adjustment, the only tools were deflation and borrowing.

The currency board arrangement required that every peso in circulation be backed by a dollar of reserves. This prevented the central bank from acting as a lender of last resort. When banks faced runs, the central bank could not provide emergency liquidity without violating the rules of convertibility. The crisis revealed that lender-of-last-resort function and fixed exchange rates are incompatible under stress.

Fiscal policy compounded the problem. Argentina's provinces had their own budgets and borrowed independently. The federal government's fiscal consolidation was undermined by provincial deficits. Debt accumulated even as the federal government attempted restraint. The political economy of intergovernmental fiscal relations prevented the adjustments that the peg required.

The IMF's role was contested. It had endorsed convertibility, provided large loans that delayed adjustment, then withheld a disbursement in December 2001 when the crisis broke. Whether earlier restructuring would have produced better outcomes, or whether the IMF's conditions contributed to the severity of the collapse, remained disputed.

Argentina's solution was unilateral and radical. It defaulted on the debt. It abandoned convertibility. It 'pesified' dollar-denominated bank loans — converting them to pesos at the pre-devaluation rate — while allowing deposits to convert at the post-devaluation rate, effectively imposing a loss on creditors and a transfer to debtors.

The immediate effect was severe. GDP fell eleven percent in 2002. Poverty rose to fifty-seven percent. The banking system was paralyzed. But with the exchange rate adjustment, exports became competitive. Brazil and China's strong demand for Argentine commodities provided an external stimulus.

Argentina negotiated a debt restructuring in 2005, exchanging its defaulted bonds for new securities at roughly thirty cents on the dollar — one of the largest haircuts in sovereign debt history. Most creditors accepted. A minority — primarily hedge funds that had bought the debt at distressed prices — refused and litigated for years.

The litigation reached the US Supreme Court. In 2014, a US court ruled that Argentina must pay the holdout creditors in full before paying the restructured bondholders. This 'vulture fund' ruling — blocking payment to the restructured bondholders — forced Argentina into a second default. The legal framework for sovereign debt restructuring remained broken.

Argentina's recovery from the 2001–2002 crisis was swift and surprising. GDP grew eight percent annually from 2003 to 2007. Poverty fell sharply. Employment recovered. The commodity boom — particularly soybean exports to China — provided the revenue base. The competitive exchange rate made manufacturing viable.

The crisis and recovery generated a contentious lesson. Argentina demonstrated that default and devaluation — the most disorderly possible exit from a debt crisis — could be followed by rapid recovery. This challenged the IMF consensus that orderly debt servicing was always preferable to default.

The institutional legacy was the debate over a sovereign debt restructuring mechanism — an orderly bankruptcy procedure for sovereign debtors analogous to corporate bankruptcy. The Argentine experience highlighted the absence of such a mechanism and the damage caused by holdout creditors. The mechanism was never created. The problem remains.

Argentina's subsequent trajectory — returning to fiscal imbalance, inflation, and eventually a new IMF program in 2018 and another crisis in 2019 — suggested that the structural reforms necessary to stabilize the economy had not been made. The immediate crisis was resolved; the underlying dysfunction persisted.

Argentina's 2001 default was, in part, the deferred consequence of the Latin American debt restructuring of the 1980s (Episode 7). The Brady bonds that had resolved the earlier crisis were among the instruments that holdout creditors used to block Argentina's 2005 restructuring. Solutions create the conditions for the next crisis.
